seasonalPresence: April to May. One generation. Males fly by day.
bestTimeOfDay: Daytime. Males fly in sunshine. Females are nocturnal.
whereToSee: Heather moorland. The fells, Grizedale, Foulshaw Moss. Males fly with that distinctive bouncing flight.
lakesPopulation: Common on heather moorland across the Lakes. April and May. Males fly by day. That orange and grey. Unmistakeable.
description: Emperor moths are the stars of spring moorland. April and May. Males fly by day with that bouncing flight. Orange and grey. The females are bigger, nocturnal. Heather moorland. The fells. Grizedale.
identification: Large moth. Male orange and grey with eye spots. Female larger, greyer. Day-flying male is the one you'll see.
tips: April and May. Heather moorland. Sunny day. Males fly with a distinctive bounce. You'll hear them before you see them sometimes.

bestTimeOfDay: Sunny mornings. They need warmth to fly.
whereToSee: Damp grassland and woodland clearings. Foulshaw Moss, Grizedale, the Borrowdale valley. Violet leaves for the caterpillars.
ukPopulation: Declining. Localised in northern and western Britain.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss and Grizedale hold populations. Damp grassland with violets. May to July. Sunny day.
description: Small pearl-bordered fritillaries need violets. The caterpillars eat them. Foulshaw Moss. Grizedale. Damp grassland. May to July. Sunny morning. They're orange and black. Fast. Follow them. They'll settle eventually.
identification: Orange and black fritillary. Pearl markings on the underside. Smaller than pearl-bordered. Violets nearby.
tips: May to July. Sunny morning. Damp grassland. Foulshaw Moss. Look for violets first. The butterflies will be there.

bestTimeOfDay: Sunny mornings. They bask on rocks.
whereToSee: Limestone grassland. The eastern Lake District. Scout Scar, Orrest Head. Rock rose is the foodplant.
lakesPopulation: Eastern Lakes. Limestone. Scout Scar. Rock rose. June to August. Small brown butterfly. Orange spots.
description: Northern brown argus is a small brown butterfly. Orange spots. Limestone grassland. Scout Scar. Orrest Head. Rock rose is the foodplant. June to August. Sunny day. They're localised. Worth the search.
identification: Small. Brown. Orange spots on the wings. Rock rose nearby. Eastern Lakes only.
tips: June to August. Eastern Lakes. Limestone. Scout Scar. Look for rock rose. The butterflies will be there.

bestTimeOfDay: Sunny mornings. They perch on bushes.
whereToSee: Heath and scrub. Grizedale, Foulshaw Moss, moorland edge. That green underside. The only green butterfly in Britain.
ukPopulation: Widespread in northern and western Britain.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale and Foulshaw Moss. Heath and scrub. April to June. Green underside. The only green butterfly. They perch. Don't fly far.
description: Green hairstreak is the only green butterfly in Britain. That underside. Grizedale. Foulshaw Moss. April to June. They perch on gorse and bilberry. Don't fly far. Easy to miss. Look for the green.
identification: Small. Green underside. Brown upperside. Perches with wings closed. The green is the giveaway.
tips: April to June. Heath and scrub. Grizedale. They perch. Move slowly. The green catches the light.
